**CS 471: Software Engineering**  
**Spring 2026**  
**Extra Credit \- Railway and Cloud Deployment (Heroku HW9 Alternative)**  
**(worth 5% of the final grade)**

Suggested Due Date: Wednesday, March 25, 2026 (at end of day)

1. # **Brief Description**

This assignment will incentivize you to get familiar with **building** and **deploying** an application (consisting of a web server interacting with a database) to the “cloud” on [railway.com](https://railway.com/).  
You can complete this assignment:

* for free (see the Free Trial option in the image below)  
* without needing a credit card

**Q: Is it ok if I complete both the [Heroku (HW9)](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1dwy3zlp0rKUIwALsh45obb0s-1phbKWxzxtN2Y40c98/edit) and this Railway assignment?**  
Yes, it’s perfectly fine if you complete either or both of these assignments, and you can even complete as much extra credit as you want from these assignments.

**NOTE:** In case railway will change their [pricing](https://railway.app/pricing) model to require a credit card (or if they will no longer offer a Free Trial), then feel free to ignore this assignment.  
The Free Trial was tested as of 2026-03-06.

The Free Trial plan should give you a $5 credit (which is more than enough to complete this extra credit assignment).  
However, the $5 credit for the Free Trial will not be sufficient if you plan to use railway.com to deploy your group project application, so you might want to choose a different cloud hosting solution, such as [heroku from HW9](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1dwy3zlp0rKUIwALsh45obb0s-1phbKWxzxtN2Y40c98/edit).  
![][image1]

2. # **Submission Template** {#submission-template}

Create a copy of the following template and start filling in the sections as you progress with the assignment:  
[CS471\_S26\_ExtraCredit\_Railway\_Heroku\_HW9\_Alternative\_Submission\_Template](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1IuCH9HgU-b0jQHmNW02K6a9kIB0_R_ajE55g4Ty5Jeo/edit?usp=sharing)

3. # **Get the sample application**

**Step 1**  
Clone on your local machine the following repository:  
[https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started](https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started)  
NOTE: 

* This is a sample Java [Spring](https://spring.io/) application developed by Heroku, which uses the gradle build system.  
* This heroku application:  
  * is used in [HW9\_Heroku](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1dwy3zlp0rKUIwALsh45obb0s-1phbKWxzxtN2Y40c98/edit)   
  * will be used in future homework assignments (e.g., Docker) and   
  * can be successfully deployed to [railway.com](https://railway.com/)

**Step 2**  
Remove the remote to the GitHub repository you cloned it from, since you will not be pushing your code to github.com/heroku, to which only heroku developers have write permissions.

**Step 3**  
In your GitHub account:

* create a new GitHub repository and   
* push the cloned repo to your newly created GitHub repository.

You will be making changes to this GitHub repo and you will deploy this repo to [railway.com](https://railway.com/).

**Summary of the steps:**  
//Step 1  
$ git clone https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started.git  
$ cd gradle-getting-started

//Step 2  
$ git remote \-v  
origin  https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started.git (fetch)  
origin  https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started.git (push)

$ git remote remove origin

//Step 3  
$ git remote add origin git@github.com:\<TBDyourGitHubUsername\>/\<TBDYourNewlyCreatedRepoName\>.git

$ git push \-u origin main                              

4. # **Deploy the application**

In [railway.com](https://railway.com/) create a new project and deploy the application from your GitHub repo.  
![][image2]

It may take up to 5 minutes to deploy the application for the first time.

Here is an [example (https://web-production-fed99.up.railway.app/)](https://web-production-fed99.up.railway.app/) of successfully deploying the java web application. 

**Q: The above URL [https://web-production-fed99.up.railway.app/](https://web-production-fed99.up.railway.app/) does not work anymore. Why?**  
Most likely the free $5 credit expired, and the app is no longer available.  
Look at the same sample application deployed to heroku at:  
[https://floating-lowlands-25777.herokuapp.com/](https://floating-lowlands-25777.herokuapp.com/)

**Troubleshooting**  
**Q: I deployed to Railway the latest version of the [gradle-getting-started](https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started) application and I see in the Railway deployment log the following error:**  
**"error: unable to access jarfile build/libs/gradle-getting-started-1.0.jar"**  
You will need to go to the settings of your web application in Railway, and under “Deploy”, change the Railway start application command from:  
java \-jar build/libs/gradle-getting-started-1.0.jar  
to  
java \-jar build/libs/java-getting-started-gradle-1.0.0-SNAPSHOT.jar  
and redeploy. 

* NOTE: This command is extracted from the [Procfile](https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started/blob/main/Procfile) (i.e., the file that tells Heroku how to start the application). Railway incorrectly guessed that the command was “java \-jar build/libs/gradle-getting-started-1.0.jar”, hence it threw the error.

![][image3]

5. # **Add a Postgres database**

Add a new Postgres database to your application:  
![][image4]

6. # **Create the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environment variable** {#create-the-jdbc_database_url-environment-variable}

See the image below for accessing the postgres database credentials in order to create the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environmental variable.  
Replace the TBD values with the actual values that were generated by [railway.com](https://railway.com/) for you when you created the Postgres database (see image below).   
Make sure there are no spaces in the value of the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environment variable.

JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL=jdbc:postgresql://TBD\_PGHOST:TBD\_PGPORT/TBD\_PGDATABASE?user=TBD\_PGUSER\&password=TBD\_PGPASSWORD

NOTE: The environment variable should look something like this, but YMMV:  
JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL=jdbc:postgresql://postgres.railway.internal:5432/railway?user=postgres\&password=randomPassword  
![][image5]

7. # **Add the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environment variable to the web server**

Add the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environment variable to the web server (not Postgres database) (see image and animation below).

![][image6]  
![][image7]

Wait for the web application to redeploy and allow the web server to connect to the postgres database.

Open the deployed web application (see image below), which will have a URL similar to:  
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/

![][image8]

Modify the URL by appending the database path at the end of the URL:  
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database  
NOTE: In the animation and image below, the old path /db was used, but the /db path is obsolete, so please use the /database path instead.  
![][image9]

![][image10]

8. # **Extra Credit: Run the application locally, while interacting with the remote database on [railway.app](https://railway.app/)** {#extra-credit:-run-the-application-locally,-while-interacting-with-the-remote-database-on-railway.app}

**Q: Which version of Java will I need to have installed locally? Will Java 8 or Java 11 work?**  
No, JDK 8 or JDK 11 do not work with this version of the application.  
You will need to have [JDK 17 or JDK 21 or JDK 25](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1vBOyitWjRQ7hNzjXWRQw7pSvjqkc2Et3DgnAa0tQ2TI/edit) on your machine.   
The main reason is that this [gradle-getting-started application uses gradle 9 (or newer)](https://github.com/heroku/gradle-getting-started/blob/7f1c8b29387bacf846e2c67a230ce135e5a36b6c/gradle/wrapper/gradle-wrapper.properties#L3), which requires [JDK 17 or newer](https://docs.gradle.org/9.0.0/release-notes.html).

**Q: Can I complete this assignment without having to run the application locally?**  
In theory, yes, you can complete this assignment without having to run the application on your local machine, but you will need to modify the source code, and if you have syntax errors or semantic errors, it will take longer to troubleshoot.

By completing these steps, you can test your changes to the application locally, before pushing the code to the GitHub repository, which in turn will trigger a new deployment on [railway.app](https://railway.app/).

**Create a public JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environmental variable**  
Similarly to [Section 6](#create-the-jdbc_database_url-environment-variable), you should create an environment variable that will use the public host and port (instead of the internal host and port) to connect to the postgres database.  
See the image below for accessing the postgres database credentials in order to create the public JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environmental variable that can be accessed from anywhere (e.g., your local computer).  
Replace the TBD values with the actual values that were generated by [railway.com](https://railway.com/) for you when you created the Postgres database.   
Make sure there are no spaces in the value of the JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environment variable.

JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL=jdbc:postgresql://TBD\_RAILWAY\_TCP\_PROXY\_DOMAIN:TBD\_RAILWAY\_TCP\_PROXY\_PORT/TBD\_PGDATABASE?user=TBD\_PGUSER\&password=TBD\_PGPASSWORD  
![][image11]

In a bash terminal, export the public JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL environmental variable.

* Notice the single quotes ' in the export command below.


$ cd \<folder-of-cloned-repo-gradle-getting-started\>  
$ export JDBC\_DATABASE\_URL='jdbc:postgresql://TBD\_RAILWAY\_TCP\_PROXY\_DOMAIN:TBD\_RAILWAY\_TCP\_PROXY\_PORT/TBD\_PGDATABASE?user=TBD\_PGUSER\&password=TBD\_PGPASSWORD'  
$ ./gradlew bootRun

The last command uses gradle to start the application that will listen to port 5000 at the URL:  
[http://localhost:5000/](http://localhost:5000/)

Take a screenshot of your browser showing a few timestamps while visiting the URL:  
[http://localhost:5000/database](http://localhost:5000/database)  
NOTE: the URL should be visible in the screenshot.

9. # **Modify the application and redeploy it to [railway.com](https://railway.com/)** {#modify-the-application-and-redeploy-it-to-railway.com}

Open the cloned application in  [IntelliJ IDEA](https://www.jetbrains.com/idea/).

Read the contents of the database() method from:  
\<folder-of-cloned-repo-gradle-getting-started\>/src/main/java/com/heroku/java/GettingStartedApplication.java

The database() method is executed on the railway servers every time there is a new request to the following URL:   
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database

Modify the database() method to mimic the functionality illustrated in this sample [java application](https://floating-lowlands-25777.herokuapp.com/database). Specifically, every time the URL is accessed:

* the **timestamp** of the request and a **randomly generated string** should be **inserted** into a table and  
* **all existing records** from that table should be retrieved and displayed in the web page

HINT: in the database() method, replace the following code:

statement.executeUpdate("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ticks (tick timestamp)");  
statement.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO ticks VALUES (now())");

with:

statement.executeUpdate(**"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS table\_timestamp\_and\_random\_string (tick timestamp, random\_string varchar(50))"**);  
statement.executeUpdate(**"INSERT INTO table\_timestamp\_and\_random\_string VALUES (now(), '"** \+ getRandomString() \+ **"')"**);  
![][image12]

After that, make the necessary adjustments in the database() method based on the new table name and the new column names. Specifically:

* create the getRandomString() method and implement it (free to use [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/), [GitHub Copilot](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1KmSNGHf9DQpyRtmAA6y12gJPxvDUUuaW_0HIrKT6EoY/edit?usp=sharing) or something similar)  
* update the SELECT statement  
* retrieve the random\_string column from the results and add it to the output

Commit your changes and push them to your GitHub repository, which will automatically deploy them to [railway.com](https://railway.com/).

Take a screenshot of your browser showing a few timestamps and randomly generated strings by visiting the URL:  
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database  
NOTE: the URL, the timestamps and the random strings should be visible in the screenshot.

![][image13]

10. #  **Add log statements in your application, and examine the logs** {#add-log-statements-in-your-application,-and-examine-the-logs}

Steps:

* In the database() method, add a System.out.println statement with your name  
* Deploy the changes to Railway  
* Access the https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database URL, which should execute the database() method  
* Examine the logs from the Railway application dashboard, where you should see the output of your print statement.

![][image14]  
![][image15]

11. #  **Extra Credit: User Input Added to Database** {#extra-credit:-user-input-added-to-database}

Create a simple HTML form at:  
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/dbinput  
that will take as a user input a string and add it to the database in the column reserved for the random string. The user input string should be retrieved from the database and visualized at the URL:  
https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database

12. #  **Extra Credit: Try a different programming language** {#extra-credit:-try-a-different-programming-language}

Choose a different [programming language](https://docs.railway.app/deploy/builds) (i.e., other than Java) and deploy the application and modify it as described in [Section 9](#modify-the-application-and-redeploy-it-to-railway.com).  
In your submission, include:

* the programming language used  
* a diff (or a screenshot) of the source code changes for generating the random string  
* the URL of the application:

https://\<random-generated-app-name-for-non-Java-language\>.up.railway.app/database

* a screenshot of your browser showing a few timestamps and randomly generated strings as described in [Section 9](#modify-the-application-and-redeploy-it-to-railway.com)

13. #  **Submission**

Generate a PDF from the [submission template (Section 2\)](#submission-template) and submit it via [Canvas](https://boisestatecanvas.instructure.com/) (see [ExtraCredit\_Railway\_Heroku\_HW9\_Alternative](https://boisestatecanvas.instructure.com/courses/46551/assignments/1579811) assignment).  
After your assignment has been graded, feel free to delete the Railway app and the Postgres database in order to not consume from your Railway credit (in case you may want to use the credits to create other apps in the future).

14. #  **Grading Rubric**

The total points for this assignment are 100 (or up to 200 with Extra Credit), and the points are distributed as follows:

| Item | Points |
| :---- | ----: |
| Link to the URL of the application (e.g., https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database) that stores some information to the database and retrieves and displays the information from the database, every time the URL is accessed (see [Section 9](#modify-the-application-and-redeploy-it-to-railway.com))  | 10 |
| Screenshot of your browser showing a few timestamps and randomly generated strings by visiting the URL https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/database  The URL should be visible in the screenshot (see [Section 9](#modify-the-application-and-redeploy-it-to-railway.com))  | 50 |
| Screenshot of the deployed application log, illustrating the output of your print statement (see [Section 10](#add-log-statements-in-your-application,-and-examine-the-logs)).  | 40 |
| **Extra credit (up to 20 points):** Screenshot of your browser showing a few timestamps while visiting the [http://localhost:5000/database](http://localhost:5000/database) URL. The URL should be visible in the screenshot (see [Section 8](#extra-credit:-run-the-application-locally,-while-interacting-with-the-remote-database-on-railway.app))  | up to 20 |
| **Extra credit (up to 30 points):** Link to the URL of the HTML input form (https://\<random-generated-app-name\>.up.railway.app/dbinput) as described in [Section 11](#extra-credit:-user-input-added-to-database).  | up to 30 |
| **Extra credit (up to 50 points):** Try a different programming language and submit all the artifacts described in [Section 12](#extra-credit:-try-a-different-programming-language).  | up to 50 |
